title: On loyalty to projects that have outgrown their original meaning
summary: Argue that continuing to serve a project, institution, or cause after it has outgrown its founding purpose raises distinct moral and practical questions about loyalty.
topic: Loyalty to projects that have exceeded or changed their original meaning
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay exploring the tension between loyalty to a project or institution and the recognition that it has evolved beyond — or even against — its original purpose. Use at least one concrete institutional example (a political party, a professional association, an NGO, a tech company, or a cultural movement you know well). Deploy inversion at least once, at least one cleft sentence, and a 'granted… even so…' concession. Avoid generic moralizing about 'staying true to your values'. Show the tension concretely. No TED-Talk opener.
hintStarters: Rarely does the moment arrive cleanly when a project ceases to be what it was…
What makes continued loyalty so difficult to judge is not betrayal but…
Granted, all institutions drift from their founding charter. Even so,…
There is a point — and it is almost never a single point — at which…

sampleText:
Rarely does the moment arrive cleanly when a project ceases to be what it was. Greenpeace in the 1970s was a loose coalition of scientists and activists willing to sail a leaky vessel into a nuclear test zone; Greenpeace in the 2020s is a professionally managed international NGO with a multi-million-euro communications budget and a Brussels lobbying office. Neither version is dishonest, exactly. But it is the long-standing volunteer of the earlier era — the person who stayed, who adapted, who rebranded along with the organisation — whose loyalty asks the hardest questions.

What makes continued loyalty so difficult to judge is not betrayal but accumulation. No single decision hollows out a founding purpose; it is the succession of reasonable-seeming accommodations — accepting the corporate donor, softening the tone for a mainstream audience, shifting from direct action to policy briefings — that eventually produces an institution that resembles its earlier self only in name. The same phenomenon is visible in political parties. The Workers' Party in Brazil, founded in the early 1980s from genuine trade-union movements, had by the mid-2000s become a governing coalition whose members held offshore accounts. Those who remained and served did not wake up one morning and choose corruption; they stayed through a thousand incremental normalisations.

It is the incremental quality of institutional drift that makes it morally harder than outright betrayal. An act of apostasy is legible; one can condemn or justify it. Complicity that arrived slowly — that is dressed as stewardship, as loyalty, as keeping the best people inside — resists clean moral accounting. The long-serving party official who tells herself she stays to prevent worse outcomes is neither obviously right nor obviously wrong. She is navigating the gap between what the institution was supposed to be and what she can observe it becoming, and that navigation has no clean map.

Granted, all institutions drift from their founding charter; expecting otherwise is to misunderstand how organisations survive. Even so, there is a meaningful difference between adaptation and co-optation. Adaptation preserves some essential continuity of purpose, even as methods and structures change. Co-optation is what happens when the institution's new purposes begin to absorb and redirect the founding energy toward ends that the founders would have resisted — when the NGO's lobbying arm begins to champion the interests of the industry it was founded to oppose, when the party's trade-union caucus becomes a vehicle for the professional class it was formed to counterbalance.

There is a point — and it is almost never a single point — at which continuing to serve lends the institution a legitimacy it has not earned on current performance. It is the founding cohort's invested identity, the residual loyalty of the original volunteers, that the successor project deploys as its credential. What was earned by the earlier people is borrowed — and the borrowing is rarely acknowledged. In this sense, the most valuable thing a long-standing member can give is not their continued service but their willingness to say, publicly and precisely, what the institution has lost.

This is not an argument for easy exit. Disillusionment as an aesthetic posture — the dramatic resignation, the memoir of moral discovery — is its own form of self-indulgence. What one might argue, instead, is that loyalty without candour is not loyalty but tacit endorsement. The person who stays and says nothing, who attends the annual conference and signs the joint communiqué, lends the institution not only their name but their silence. That silence — structured, repeated, increasingly visible to those outside — is itself a kind of formative investment in whatever the project is now becoming, regardless of what it once was.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after a negative adverb for formal opening
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu mở 'Rarely does the moment arrive cleanly when a project ceases to be what it was.' đảo trợ động từ 'does' trước chủ ngữ 'the moment' sau trạng từ phủ định 'Rarely'. Đây là cấu trúc đảo ngữ C1 tạo hiệu ứng trang trọng và khiến câu mang sức nặng hơn câu khẳng định thông thường.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The opening 'Rarely does the moment arrive cleanly when a project ceases to be what it was.' inverts auxiliary 'does' before the subject 'the moment' after the negative adverb 'Rarely' — a C1 inversion that opens with formal gravitas, carrying more weight than a plain affirmative statement.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to isolate a key agent
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is the founding cohort's invested identity, the residual loyalty of the original volunteers, that the successor project deploys as its credential.' dùng cấu trúc tách 'It is X that…' để đặt trọng tâm vào X — nguồn lực mà dự án kế thừa khai thác. Dấu phẩy mở rộng thêm 'the residual loyalty of the original volunteers' là một apposition trang trọng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The cleft 'It is the founding cohort's invested identity, the residual loyalty of the original volunteers, that the successor project deploys as its credential.' places 'It is X that…' focus on the resource the successor project exploits. The comma-bracketed apposition adds formal elaboration typical of C1 argumentation.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hiếm khi khoảnh khắc đến một cách rõ ràng khi một dự án không còn là những gì nó từng là. Greenpeace vào những năm 1970 là một liên minh lỏng lẻo của các nhà khoa học và nhà hoạt động sẵn sàng lái một con tàu rỉ sét vào vùng thử nghiệm hạt nhân; Greenpeace vào những năm 2020 là một tổ chức phi chính phủ quốc tế được quản lý chuyên nghiệp với ngân sách truyền thông hàng triệu euro. Không phiên bản nào thực sự thiếu trung thực. Nhưng chính người tình nguyện lâu năm của kỷ nguyên trước — người đã ở lại, thích nghi, thay đổi thương hiệu cùng tổ chức — lòng trung thành của người đó đặt ra những câu hỏi khó nhất.

Điều khiến sự trung thành liên tục khó đánh giá không phải là sự phản bội mà là sự tích lũy. Không một quyết định đơn lẻ nào làm rỗng đi mục đích sáng lập; đó là chuỗi những nhượng bộ có vẻ hợp lý — chấp nhận nhà tài trợ doanh nghiệp, làm mềm giọng điệu cho khán giả đại chúng, chuyển từ hành động trực tiếp sang tóm tắt chính sách — cuối cùng tạo ra một tổ chức chỉ giống phiên bản trước của nó về tên gọi.

Chính tính chất dần dần của sự trôi dạt tổ chức khiến nó khó hơn về mặt đạo đức so với sự phản bội thẳng thừng. Sự đồng lõa đến từ từ — được nguỵ trang thành trách nhiệm quản lý, lòng trung thành, giữ những người tốt nhất ở bên trong — chống lại sự tính toán đạo đức rõ ràng.

Cứ cho là mọi tổ chức đều trôi dạt khỏi điều lệ sáng lập; kỳ vọng ngược lại là hiểu sai cách tổ chức tồn tại. Dù vậy, có một sự khác biệt có ý nghĩa giữa sự thích nghi và sự đồng hoá. Sự thích nghi giữ lại một sự liên tục thiết yếu của mục đích. Sự đồng hoá xảy ra khi các mục đích mới của tổ chức bắt đầu hấp thụ và chuyển hướng năng lượng sáng lập sang những mục tiêu mà những người sáng lập sẽ cưỡng lại.

Có một điểm — và gần như không bao giờ là một điểm duy nhất — mà việc tiếp tục phục vụ trao cho tổ chức một tính hợp pháp mà nó chưa đạt được dựa trên hiệu suất hiện tại. Chính bản sắc đã đầu tư của nhóm sáng lập, lòng trung thành còn sót lại của những tình nguyện viên ban đầu, mà dự án kế thừa triển khai như bằng chứng của mình. Điều được kiếm được bởi những người trước đây được vay mượn — và việc vay mượn đó hiếm khi được thừa nhận.

Đây không phải là lập luận ủng hộ việc rời đi dễ dàng. Người ta có thể lập luận rằng lòng trung thành không có sự thẳng thắn không phải là trung thành mà là sự tán thành ngầm. Người ở lại và không nói gì, tham dự hội nghị thường niên và ký tuyên bố chung, không chỉ cho mượn tên mà còn cho mượn cả sự im lặng. Sự im lặng đó — có cấu trúc, lặp đi lặp lại, ngày càng rõ ràng hơn với những người bên ngoài — là một sự đầu tư định hình cho bất cứ điều gì dự án đang trở thành.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly applies inversion after a negative adverb?
options: Rarely the moment arrives cleanly when a project changes.
Rarely does the moment arrive cleanly when a project ceases to be what it was.
The moment rarely does arrive cleanly.
Rarely, the moment does not arrive cleanly.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Rarely' at the start, the auxiliary 'does' must precede the subject 'the moment'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'mission creep' describe in the context of the essay?
options: A deliberate decision to change an organisation's goals
The gradual expansion of an institution's activities beyond its original purpose
A sudden crisis that forces an organisation to change
An employee who slowly undermines the company's mission
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes how reasonable-seeming accommodations cumulatively shift an institution away from its original purpose — classic mission creep.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the difference between 'adaptation' and 'co-optation' as the essay defines them?
options: Adaptation is good; co-optation is bad, with no further distinction.
Adaptation preserves essential continuity of purpose; co-optation redirects founding energy toward ends the founders would have resisted.
Adaptation changes the structure; co-optation changes the name.
Adaptation is slow; co-optation happens overnight.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay distinguishes them precisely: adaptation keeps the core purpose; co-optation turns founding energy against the original aims.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the paragraph beginning 'Granted, all institutions drift…'

mcQuestions.prompt: In the essay, what does the person who stays and signs the joint communiqué contribute beyond their name?
options: Their expertise
Their financial resources
Their silence — which functions as tacit endorsement
Their connections to the founding cohort
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says 'lends the institution not only their name but their silence. That silence… is itself a kind of formative investment'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the phrase 'not only their name but their silence'.

options: An organisation moving its offices to a new city
The gradual, often unnoticed shift of an institution away from its founding purpose
A financial crisis that causes an organisation to restructure
The process by which staff turnover changes a company's culture overnight
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Institutional drift' is the cumulative, incremental movement away from original purpose — not sudden or intentional.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay claim about the Workers' Party in Brazil?
options: It was founded as a corrupt institution from the beginning.
Its members deliberately chose corruption on a single day.
Members stayed through a thousand incremental normalisations, not a single act of betrayal.
It successfully maintained its founding trade-union values throughout its governance.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay specifically says 'they stayed through a thousand incremental normalisations' — emphasising the accumulated drift, not a dramatic turn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft to focus on the key agent?
options: The successor project deploys the founding cohort's invested identity.
It is the founding cohort's invested identity, the residual loyalty of the original volunteers, that the successor project deploys as its credential.
Founding identity and residual loyalty are deployed by the successor project.
What the founding cohort has is identity, which the successor project uses.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X that Y' is a cleft that foregrounds X — here, the founding identity being borrowed.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is … that …' structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: The essay calls 'disillusionment as an aesthetic posture' self-indulgent. What does this mean?
options: Being disillusioned is always wrong.
Performing dramatic resignation for its own moral beauty is a form of self-indulgence rather than principled action.
Disillusionment is a useful emotional tool.
People who are disillusioned care too much about aesthetics.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay separates genuine principled exit from the theatrical gesture of public resignation as self-expression.
mcQuestions.hint: Focus on 'aesthetic posture' — performing for an audience.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best fits the essay's register for describing continued participation despite doubts?
options: hanging in there
staying on board
residual loyalty of long-standing members
not wanting to quit
mcQuestions.explanation: C uses formal, abstract language consistent with the essay's C1 register.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the most precise and formal option.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's conclusion about the most valuable thing a long-standing member can offer?
options: Continued service and attendance at conferences
Financial contributions to the institution
Their willingness to say publicly and precisely what the institution has lost
Recruitment of new members to revitalise the founding vision
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'the most valuable thing a long-standing member can give is not their continued service but their willingness to say, publicly and precisely, what the institution has lost'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the word 'valuable' and the contrast with 'continued service'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition correctly signals a concession followed by a counter-argument?
options: Furthermore, all institutions drift. In addition, this is expected.
Granted, all institutions drift from their founding charter. Even so, there is a meaningful difference.
Although institutions drift, granted they are even so different.
Even so, all institutions drift. Granted, this is a difference.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted… Even so…' is the canonical concede-and-counter pattern; the order must be concession first, then counter.
mcQuestions.hint: The concession ('Granted') must come before the pivot ('Even so').

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'complicity that arrived slowly' mean in the essay?
options: Corruption that was planned in advance
Moral co-responsibility that accumulated through incremental decisions rather than a single choice
A legal contract signed over several years
The slow arrival of new members who are corrupt
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay emphasises that complicity is not a single act but an accumulation of small accommodations.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'arrived slowly' as 'built up over time'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'hollow out' mean in the phrase 'No single decision hollows out a founding purpose'?
options: Completely destroys a building
Gradually removes the substance or meaning from something while keeping its outward form
Loudly announces the end of something
Rebuilds something from scratch
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Hollow out' means to remove the interior content — here, the authentic purpose — while the shell (name, brand) remains.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of something that looks intact but is empty inside.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which is the best near-paraphrase of 'loyalty without candour is not loyalty but tacit endorsement'?
options: If you are loyal, you must always speak your mind.
Staying silent while remaining loyal is, in practice, an unspoken form of approval.
Candour is more important than loyalty in all situations.
Tacit endorsement is a legitimate form of loyalty.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that silence within a flawed institution functions as implicit approval, not genuine loyalty.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that equates silence with implicit approval.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay say the long-standing volunteer 'asks the hardest questions'?
options: Because they are the most critical of the institution's work
Because their continued loyalty, despite witnessing the drift, challenges the moral line between adaptation and complicity
Because they have been members for longer than anyone else
Because they regularly attend board meetings and ask difficult questions
mcQuestions.explanation: Their continued presence, despite knowing the change, embodies the central moral tension the essay explores.
mcQuestions.hint: Their situation poses the question: at what point does staying become endorsing?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of an organisation, project, or cause you have been part of — or observed closely. At what point, if any, did you notice it had changed from what it originally stood for? What did people who stayed do, and what did their staying mean?
